facebook.texto: I quit Mounjaro 14 months ago and my husband finally asked what my secret is for keeping it off. Last Tuesday at 11:47 PM, I found my husband sitting on our bathroom floor, holding my box of injection pens, tears streaming down his face. "I can't watch you go through this again," he whispered. He wasn't talking about the injections. He was talking about what happened the last two times I stopped. ======== See, I've been on the shots three times now. >>First time: Lost 38 pounds.

analise_criativa.avatar: Middle-aged woman (Sarah Collins) shown in profile avatar in a kitchen setting, paired with an image of a handwritten results chart showing weight loss across three rounds of Mounjaro injections — personal, relatable, real-person framing
analise_criativa.editing: Static image ad using a handwritten notecard as the visual hero — raw, unpolished, authenticity-driven aesthetic. No filters or design polish. Feels like organic social content rather than a produced ad.
analise_criativa.observation: The handwritten chart showing 'Round 1: 38lbs, Round 2: 41lbs, Round 3...' with a note about 9 months off the shots is a strong pattern interrupt. The emotional bathroom floor scene in the copy creates unusually high dramatic tension. Facebook-only placement suggests a more conservative or test-phase deployment.
analise_criativa.highlight: The handwritten notecard visual is a high-curiosity hook — it implies a real personal journey with documented proof without showing a product. The 'quit Mounjaro and kept it off' angle is a powerful counter-narrative to the dominant GLP-1 conversation. Strong scroll-stop potential for audiences tired of medication dependency stories.

facebook.texto: I was diagnosed with obesity at 42. I'm 51 now. Turns out I never had obesity at all. For nine years, I "managed" a condition I didn't actually have. Nine years of weight-loss shots - Saxenda, then Wegovy, then Ozempic - that dropped fifteen pounds and then stalled, and handed every pound back the second I stopped. Nine years of phentermine my doctor prescribed "to jump-start things" - even though all it did was make my heart pound while the scale stayed put. Nine years of being handed a new pla

analise_criativa.avatar: Woman in her early 50s (Sophie Hartwell) shown in a close-up profile avatar, with the ad image featuring a cropped mid-section of a woman's torso showing a slim waist — implied body transformation without explicit before/after framing
analise_criativa.editing: Minimalist static image — cropped body shot of a slim midsection against a neutral background. Clean and understated. The avatar photo shows an attractive, relatable woman. No heavy design elements or text overlays on the image.
analise_criativa.observation: The body midsection shot is borderline — it passes the visual filter as it clearly implies weight loss transformation and would make a woman curious about how she achieved that result. The copy angle of 'misdiagnosed with obesity for 9 years' is an unusually bold and legally risky claim that could trigger platform review. Multi-platform distribution (Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, Threads) suggests confidence in the creative.
analise_criativa.highlight: The '9 years of being lied to by the medical system' narrative is a powerful anti-establishment hook with high emotional resonance for women 40-55 who feel failed by conventional weight loss approaches. The midsection visual paired with that copy creates strong click-curiosity. Worth testing the copy angle in video format with a talking head.

repeated_creatives.description: Both ads use a named female persona with a profile avatar, personal narrative framing around GLP-1 injectable drugs (Mounjaro/Ozempic/Saxenda/Wegovy), and an implied body transformation visual — one using a handwritten chart, one using a cropped torso. The storytelling structure is near-identical: long-form confessional, specific timestamps or ages for authenticity, and a dramatic revelation hook. Likely variants of the same winning creative framework if not the same underlying video/image template.

frequent_avatars.type: Middle-aged woman (40s–50s) who used GLP-1 injectables (Mounjaro, Ozempic, Saxenda, Wegovy) for weight loss and is now seeking or found a natural/sustainable alternative — health-conscious, slightly regretful of pharmaceutical route, relatable and credible

facebook_cross_analysis.dominant_mechanism: Natural alternatives to GLP-1 receptor agonist injectables (Mounjaro/Ozempic) — positioning supplements as delivering similar appetite suppression or metabolic effects without the risks, cost, or dependency of prescription weight-loss drugs
facebook_cross_analysis.emerging_angle: Misdiagnosis narrative — Sophie Hartwell's ad claims she was incorrectly diagnosed with obesity for nine years and medicated unnecessarily, reframing the target audience not just as people who want to lose weight but as potential victims of a broken medical system, creating distrust of pharmaceutical solutions and strong openness to natural alternatives
facebook_cross_analysis.market_signal: The US market is aggressively testing GLP-1 drug fatigue creatives — targeting existing or former Ozempic/Mounjaro users with personal confession-style ads that validate quitting injectables and pivot toward natural supplement alternatives, using hyper-specific autobiographical details to manufacture trust and virality
